coredata.dc:title: Carbonized polymer dots-based molecular imprinting: An adsorbent with enhanced selectivity for highly efficient recognition and removal of ceftiofur sodium from complex samples

coredata.dc:description: Highly selective removal of residual cephalosporin antibiotics from complex systems is crucial for human health and ecological environment protection. Herein, a newly molecularly imprinted polymer adsorbent (CPDs-NH2@MIP) with enhanced selectivity for ceftiofur sodium (CTFS) was developed by using the special carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-NH2) as functional monomer. The CPDs-NH2 has a nano-spherical structure and functionalized groups (CC, −NH2) via the incomplete carbonization polymerization of citric acid, acrylamide and ethylenediamine, which can accurately interact with CTFS by overcoming steric hindrance, resulting in more precisely imprinted sites and reducing non-imprinted regions in MIP. The presented CPDs-NH2@MIP exhibited excellent adsorption capacity for CTFS (68.62 mg g−1), achieving equilibrium within 10 min, and highly selectivity in mixed solution containing five coexisting substances, with an imprinted factor (5.61). Compared with commercial adsorbents and MIPs prepared with traditional chain functional monomers, the CPDs-NH2@MIP showed significant advantage in selective recognition and separation of target. Analysis of microstructure and mechanism proved that usage of the spherical functional monomer generated precise imprinting sites and dense structure in CPDs-NH2@MIP, which effectively enhanced the selectivity in complex system combined with hydrogen bonding interaction. The idea of designing and using spherical functional monomer will promote the practicality of molecularly imprinted polymer adsorbents.

head.ce:title: Carbonized polymer dots-based molecular imprinting: An adsorbent with enhanced selectivity for highly efficient recognition and removal of ceftiofur sodium from complex samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly selective removal of residual cephalosporin antibiotics from complex systems is crucial for human health and ecological environment protection. Herein, a newly molecularly imprinted polymer adsorbent (CPDs-NH2@MIP) with enhanced selectivity for ceftiofur sodium (CTFS) was developed by using the special carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-NH2) as functional monomer. The CPDs-NH2 has a nano-spherical structure and functionalized groups (CC, −NH2) via the incomplete carbonization polymerization of citric acid, acrylamide and ethylenediamine, which can accurately interact with CTFS by overcoming steric hindrance, resulting in more precisely imprinted sites and reducing non-imprinted regions in MIP. The presented CPDs-NH2@MIP exhibited excellent adsorption capacity for CTFS (68.62 mg g−1), achieving equilibrium within 10 min, and highly selectivity in mixed solution containing five coexisting substances, with an imprinted factor (5.61). Compared with commercial adsorbents and MIPs prepared with traditional chain functional monomers, the CPDs-NH2@MIP showed significant advantage in selective recognition and separation of target. Analysis of microstructure and mechanism proved that usage of the spherical functional monomer generated precise imprinting sites and dense structure in CPDs-NH2@MIP, which effectively enhanced the selectivity in complex system combined with hydrogen bonding interaction. The idea of designing and using spherical functional monomer will promote the practicality of molecularly imprinted polymer adsorbents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are frequently utilized in human and animal for curative or preventive therapy [1–4]. However, excessive antibiotic usage can pollute the environment, and unregulated antibiotic exposure increases the likelihood of allergies and bacterial resistance, posing a serious threat to human health and ecological environment quality [5–7]. Ceftiofur sodium (CTFS), a broad-spectrum cephalosporin antibiotic, has been widely used in serious bacterial infections in animal husbandry and salmonella coli in poultry, and that the widespread use of CTFS may lead to residues in meat, food, and water, thereby endangering human health [8–11]. Because of the low content and complex media of the residual antibiotics in real samples, the appropriate sample pre-treatment is required to avoid the interference of sample matrices and increase concentration. Hence, it is critical to develop sample pretreatment technique with highly selectivity for recognizing and removal of these pollutants.

Solid phase extraction (SPE), as an effective sample pretreatment method, has been extensively used due to its advantages of easy operation, high enrichment coefficient, and high extraction efficiency [12]. Especially the combination with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with specific recognition performance [13–15], the recognition and separation ability of SPE has been significantly improved and has received widespread attention in the removal of targets. However, because of the limited imprinting sites and the existence of non-specific recognition in MIPs, the selective recognition ability of currently used MIPs is usually unsatisfactory, with an imprinting factor only within 1–3 [16–18]. When applied to complex system, coexisting impurities can affect the performance of precise recognition and separation for target pollutants, thereby limiting its practicality in environment.

During the procedure of preparing MIPs, functional monomer plays a key role as they can establish recognition sites by forming complexes with template [19–21]. The matching degree and binding intensity between functional monomers and template molecules not only influence the affinity of MIPs for target, but also affect the accuracy of recognition sites, thereby determining the selectivity of MIPs [22,23]. So far, the functional monomers used for the preparation of MIPs are mostly chain-like chemical structures, including methacrylic acid, vinyl pyridine, acrylic acid, and 2-acrylamido-2-methylpropane sulfonic acid etc. The functional groups in these chain-like monomers have different spatial orientations, resulting in spatial resistance, making it difficult to accurately match with template molecules, and the functional groups cannot be fully utilized [24]. Thus, the monomers that fail to form functional monomer-template molecule complexes may exhibit disorderly arrangement or self-polymerization, leading to the appearance of non-imprinted regions in MIPs [25,26]. These non-imprinted regions mixed in the imprinted material also have active groups, which can non-specifically adsorb the coexisting targets and impurities, thereby decreasing the recognition specificity of MIPs [26–29]. Therefore, it is of great significance to design and synthesize functional monomers with novel chemical structures for obtaining imprinted materials with more precise recognition sites, decreasing the generation of non-imprinted regions, and improving the selectivity of MIPs in real complex matrices.

Carbon dots (CDs) are spherical or nearly spherical fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm. The sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon atoms of CDs resulted in excellent optical properties and stability, and so they are widely applied in the fields of sensing [30,31], biological imaging [32,33], optoelectronic devices [34–38] etc. However, the structural composition and types of surface functional groups of CDs are related to the carbon source selected and synthesis method [39,40]. So, by adjusting the preparation system, it is expected to develop new properties of CDs and expand their application fields. It was found that a special type of CDs, carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with spherical core-shell structure, can be obtained through the incomplete carbonization process of carbon source [41–43]. By selecting suitable carbon sources, the shell of CPDs can contain multiple types of functional groups, such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, ring structure and vinyl double bond structure etc., which not only can form hydrogen bonding, electrostatic, π-π interactions with the target, but can form polymers via the polymerization reaction [44,45]. Thus, CPDs can become the preferred novel functional monomers for preparing imprinting materials with excellent recognition performance [46].

CTFS is a compound with carbonyl, hydroxyl, amino, and cyclic structures, which is highly likely to bind with CPDs containing functionalized groups, such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl etc. via non-covalent interaction including hydrogen bonding, electrostatic forces, and other forces [47]. Additionally, the nano-spherical structure of CPDs can break through spatial hindrance and allow the functional groups with consistent spatial orientation on its surface to precisely bind with CTFS, forming dense and ordered imprinted cavities in polymer. At the same time, this combination effectively reduces the generation of non-imprinting areas. And so the obtained imprinted material can more accurately identify the target from complex systems [27–29], thereby significantly improving the selectivity of the imprinted polymer.

Herein, in this work, citric acid, acrylamide containing vinyl double bond (CC) and ethylenediamine containing functional group (–NH2) were selected as precursors to prepare amino-functionalized CPDs (CPDs-NH2) through the incomplete carbonization process by using one-step hydrothermal method. The CPDs-NH2 has a spherical core and is rich in amino groups on the outside, which was used as a newly functional monomer to synthesize CTFS imprinted material CPDs-NH2@MIP on the surface of poly styrene divinylbenzene particles (PS-DVB) in water media. Combined the characterization of microstructure and morphology and adsorption properties, the mechanism of enhanced selectivity of CPDs-NH2@MIP towards target was explored. Further, seven real environmental samples were selected to discuss the practical performance of CPDs-NH2@MIP as a newly SPE adsorbent for removing pollutants.

Ceftiofur sodium (CTFS) was obtained from North China Pharmaceutical Group Co. Ltd. (Shijiazhuang, China). Citric acid (≥99.5%), Ethylenediamine (98%), Acrylamide (AM, 99%), Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC, 96%), Oxytetracycline (OTC, 95%), Sulfadiazine (SZ, 98%), Sulfamethoxazole (SMZ, 98%), Bisphenol A (BPA, 99%), Bisphenol B (BPB, 98%), Azithromycin (AM, 99%), Atrazine (ATZ, 98%), Carbamazepine (CBZ, 98%), chloramphenicol (CPL, 98%), Methacrylic acid (MAA, 99%), Acrylic acid (AA, 99%), and 2-Acrylamido-2-methylpropane sulfonic acid (AMPS, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Poly styrene–divinylbenzene particles (PS-DVB, 40–60 µm) were supplied by Shanghai Boshi Biotech. Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide was bought from J&K Chem. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 99%) by Beijing Chemical Industry Co. (Beijing, China). Chromatographic grade of methanol was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA) and ultrapure water was purchased from Wahaha Co. Ltd (Hangzhou, China). Four types of SPE columns, CNWBOND Si (60 mg, 3 mL), CNW Poly-Sery HLB (60 mg, 3 mL), and CNWBOND LC-C18 (60 mg, 3 mL) were purchased from (ANPEL, Shanghai, China), Oasis HLB (60 mg, 3 mL) was from Waters (Milford, MA, USA).
See Supplementary Material.

In brief, citric acid (0.9607 g, 0.005 mol), ethylenediamine (334 μL, 0.005 mol), acrylamide (0.3550 g, 0.005 mol), and 10 mL of deionized water were mixed followed by ultrasonication for 10 min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 12 h at 120, 150, 180, 200, and 220°C, respectively. After chilled to 25°C, the obtained brown aqueous solution containing the CPDs-NH2 was purified using a 0.22 µm membrane. Dialysis was employed to eliminate unreacted precursors, utilizing a 1000 Da dialysis bag for roughly 24 h. Finally, the CPDs-NH2 solutions were obtained for use. Fig. 1A presents a schematic diagram of the synthesis of CPDs-NH2.

The imprinted material preparation procedure is as follows: 0.1360 g (0.25 mmol) of CTFS (template molecule), 200 μL of CPDs-NH2 (functional monomer), 0.2 g of PS-DVB (carrier), and 20 mL of ultrapure water were dispersed into a 100 mL polyethylene reaction. The pre-polymerization was conducted at 30°C for 4 h in a water bath oscillator. Following this, 0.7708 g (5.00 mmol) of N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide (crosslinker), and 10 mg of azobisisobutyronitrile (initiator) were dissolved in the aforementioned solution. The resulting mixture was subjected to deoxygenation for 10 min using nitrogen. The mixture was polymerized for 24 h at 60°C in a water bath oscillator by shaking at a speed of 300 rpm. Soxhlet extraction was employed to eliminate the template molecule from the resultant product using a 9:1 (V/V) mixture of acetic acid and methanol. Finally, after 24 h of vacuum drying at 60°C, CPDs-NH2@MIP was finally obtained. The CPDs-NH2@NIP preparation process is identical to that of CPDs-NH2@MIP except that template CTFS is not added. A schematic diagram of the CPDs-NH2 @MIP preparation is shown in Fig. 1B.

The adsorption kinetics of CTFS onto CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP were investigated. Initially, 10 mg of CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP was dissolved in 10 mL CTFS aqueous solution (20 mg L−1), respectively, followed by water bath oscillation at 25℃ for different time intervals (1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 55, 70, 90, 110, 135, 160, 200, and 240 min). Subsequently, HPLC (see Supplementary Material) was utilized to measure the concentration of CTFS in supernatant, and the adsorption capacity (Q) was calculated according to the following equation [10]:
(1)

For the investigate of adsorption isotherms, 10 mg of the as-prepared imprinted polymers were dispersed in 10 mL CTFS aqueous solution, with different concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 200 mg L−1, and incubated 120 min at 25, 35, and 45°C. After achieving adsorption equilibrium, the residual solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane and analyzed by using HPLC.

Eleven contaminants (Fig. S1), including ceftiofur sodium (CTFS), sulfadiazine (SZ), sulfamethoxazole (SMZ), carbamazepine (CBZ), bisphenol A (BPA), atrazine (ATZ), bisphenol B (BPB), oxytetracycline (OTC), chloramphenicol (CPL), azithromycin (AM), and tetracycline (TC) were selected as references to evaluate the selectivity of CPDs-NH2@MIP via single and competitive adsorption experiments. Specifically, 10 mg CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP were separately added into 20 mg L−1 aqueous solution (10 mL) of SZ, SMZ, BPA, AM, TC, or OTC. Besides, the competitive selectivity was also explored, and 10 mg CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP were added into a 10 mL mixture containing 20 mg L−1 of CTFS and other five contaminants (CPL, CBZ, BPA, ATZ, and BPB), respectively. All the above solutions were oscillated at 25°C for 2 h, and the residual concentrations were quantified using HPLC, and the rebinding capacity Q (mg g−1), imprinting factor (IF), and selectivity factor (α) were calculated using Eq.1, Eq.2, and Eq.3, respectively [48]:
(2)

Four water samples (river water, lake water, influent and effluent of sewage treatment plant) collected from Anyang city and three food samples (milk, pork, and chicken) from a local supermarket of Anyang city (Henan, China) were selected to evaluate the anti-interference and selectivity of the CPDs-NH2 @MIP as a SPE adsorbent in complex matrix of environmental samples, and each sample without and with spiked were firstly pretreated.

For subsequent use, 100 mL of each water sample was firstly spiked with CTFS (10 μg L−1, 50 μg L−1, 100 μg L−1), and then filtered using a 0.45 µm membrane. Milk sample (10 mL) was also spiked with CTFS (10 μg L−1, 50 μg L−1, 100 μg L−1) prior to mixing with 4 mL acidified acetonitrile (pH = 4.0) followed by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 10 min to precipitate the protein, the supernatant was then collected. After three repeated extraction process, all supernatant were mixed and evaporated till dryness. Finally, the residue was redissolved in 10 mL of pure water for use. Pork and chicken samples (5 g) were firstly separately homogenized followed by spiking CTFS (10, 50, and 100 μg kg−1), and the samples was placed into polypropylene tubes and 5 mL of acetonitrile was then added into them, respectively. After thoroughly mixing, the solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min and then collected the supernatant. The procedure was repeated three times and all collected supernatant was gathered and evaporated to nearly dryness. Finally, 10 mL of pure water was used to dissolve the residue, and the solution was filtered by a 0.22 µm membrane before use. After optimizing the extraction parameters, all the above pretreated samples were separately passed through the CPDs-NH2@MIP-SPE column (see Supplementary Materials). Simultaneously, the other three SPE column packed with MIPs prepared with traditional chain functional monomers including methacrylic acid (MAA), acrylic acid (AA), and 2-acrylamido-2-methylpropanesulfonic acid (AMPS) (MAA@MIP, AA@MIP, and AMPS@MIP) were separately employed to value their extraction performance (see Supplementary Materials).

Desorption and regeneration studies were conducted to assess the reusability of CPDs-NH2@MIP. In summary, a 10 mL sample of the CTFS aqueous solution (1 mg L−1) was filtered via the self-made CPDs-NH2@MIP-SPE column and then rinsed with 1 mL of acetonitrile. The HPLC method was used to quantify the concentration of CTFS in the effluent. The regeneration efficacy of the as-prepared CPDs-NH2@MIP-SPE was subsequently assessed by conducting 10 cycles of the adsorption–desorption process.

In order to obtain the best functional monomer, the effect of preparation temperature (120, 150, 180, 200, and 220°C) of CPDs-NH2 was firstly optimized. Fig. S3 showed that both the adsorption capacity and imprinting factor of the imprinting material using CPDs-NH2 prepared at 180°C as functional monomer are optimal, while lower and higher temperatures are not conducive to the formation of CPDs-NH2. The possible reason was that at lower temperatures, the degree of carbonization was insufficient, which can prevent the formation of spherical structure of CPDs-NH2. Conversely, higher temperatures will promote the completely carbonization, leading to a decrease of functional groups on CPDs-NH2, which is averse to participating in polymerization reaction. And so the CPDs-NH2 produced at 180°C with best spherical structure and functionalized groups was chosen in the following tests.

Next, the parameters including molar/dosage ratio of the template to CPDs-NH2 (1:100, 1:200, 1:400, 1:600, and 1:800 μL) (Fig. S4), molar ratio of template to crosslinking agent (1:0, 1:5, 1:10, 1:15, 1:20, 1:25, and 1:30) (Fig. S5), and dose of the carrier (0.10–0.30 g) (Fig. S6) were investigated, respectively. It was found that the best CPDs-NH2@MIP was obtained when the molar ratio of template molecule to CPDs-NH2 and cross-linker was set to 1:200 μL:20 using a carrier of 0.20 g. Under these conditions, the imprinting factor (IF = 5.42) and adsorption capacity (Q = 7.03 mg g−1 ) of CPDs-NH2@MIP for CTFS were more than twice that of MAA@MIP, AA@MIP and AMPS@MIP prepared with traditional chain-like functional monomers (IF = 1.42–2.27, Q = 2.39–2.68 mg g−1) (Fig. 1C). This result indicated that the spherical monomer CPDs-NH2 can more flexibly and precisely bind to imprinted molecule, forming a large number of effective binding sites in CPDs-NH2@MIP that significantly improve the affinity and selectivity towards template molecule. Relatively speaking, those traditional functional monomers have chain-like structures, such as MAA, AA, and AMPS (Fig. 1D). When they combine with pollutants with complex molecular structures, the significant spatial resistance can prevent the complete binding of functional groups in monomers to template molecules. Also, those unbound functional groups in chain-like monomers will self-polymerize to form more ineffective non-imprinted regions, resulting in a limited number of imprinted sites and a large number of ineffective pores in MIPs, leading to inaccurate matching with targets and poor recognition performance [74].

TEM images ( Fig. 2A) showed that the CPDs-NH2 had evenly spaced nanodots with a particle diameter of 2.29 ± 0.06 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images in Fig. 2B show a lattice-fringe spacing of 0.21 nm in CPDs-NH2, consistent with the lattice spacing of the (100) facet in graphitic carbon, indicating that these nanodots belong to carbon dots [49]. The XRD pattern displays the characteristic peak of typical CPDs-NH2 centered around 2θ = 20◦, which is attributed to polymer chains and highly disordered carbon (Fig. 2C) [50]. Also, the photoluminescence spectra (Fig. 2D) revealed that the synthesized CPDs-NH2 exhibit a conspicuous excitation-independent multicolor emission feature. All these features are completely different from the original carbon source, verifying that carbonization process changed the structure and performance of carbon source, and the CPDs-NH2 is successfully synthesized.

Further, the FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2E of original carbon sources displayed there were absorption bands at 3357 cm−1, 3490 cm−1, 1673 cm−1, 1580 cm−1, and 1130 cm−1, which were ascribed to the stretching of –OH, N–H [51], CC, CO, and C–N [52], respectively. However, there were no –OH and CO bands in CPDs-NH2, and the N–H and CC characteristic peaks bule-shifted from 3490 cm−1 to 3201 cm−1, and 1673 cm−1 to and 1658 cm−1 (curve Ⅳ), respectively [53]. This result indicated that CPDs-NH2 was originated from the incomplete carbonization of precursor. In addition, the XPS spectra showed that the composition of CPDs-NH2 was remarkably affected by the carbonization temperature. Compared with the physical mixture of carbon sources before carbonization treatment, each full XPS spectrum in Fig. 2F exhibited three characteristic peaks at 284.7 eV (C1s), 400.4 eV (N1s), and 532.5 eV (O1s) [54], respectively, and the element content of C increased from 55.69% to 72.11%, while O decreased from 34.01% to 17.46%, and N was stable with the increase of carbonization temperature ranged from 120–220°C (Fig. 2G), suggesting that the degree of dehydration, condensation, and crosslinking during the carbonization process is greatly affected by temperature. The CPDs-NH2 prepared at 180°C (CPDs-NH2-180) has a higher proportion of CC (34.60%) and C–N (18.19%) (Table S3), indicating that this temperature is benefit for the formation of spherical structure and retaining a large number of functional groups in CPDs-NH2, which was the best functional monomer.

The surface morphologies of PS-DVB, CPDs-NH2@MIP, and CPDs-NH2@NIP were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 3A, the PS-DVB carrier is spherical with a smooth surface of 100 µm. In contrast, the dimensions of CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2 @NIP (Figs. 3B and 3C) did not change significantly, but the surface became rough and the imprinting layer could be clearly seen. The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 3D showed that the CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP have significantly different characteristic peaks compared to the carrier. Notably, the broad peaks at 3271 cm−1 and 1646 cm−1 are attributed to the N–H and CC stretching vibrations of CPDs-NH2, respectively [55]. The vibration absorption peaks of CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP are identical, showing that the template molecules of CTFS are thoroughly washed off from the imprinted polymer. All the full XPS spectra in Fig. 3E had characteristic peaks at 281 eV and 529 eV, which was corresponding to C1s and O1s of PS-DVB, respectively. However, compared to the carrier PS-DVB, the observation of a novel signal at 400.1 eV for CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP was attributed to N1s, potentially originating from the N group present in CPDs-NH2 and the crosslinker, indicating that the functional monomer and crosslinker were successful polymerized in imprinted material.

The thermal stability of PS-DVB, CPDs-NH2@MIP, and CPDs-NH2@NIP was characterized in nitrogen environment. As depicted in Fig. 3F, PS-DVB demonstrates good stability below 400°C, while CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP exhibit a minor loss of approximately 15.03 wt% at 360°C, potentially attributable to the removal of water and small molecules. When beyond 470°C, the mass loss increased to approximately 73.20 wt%, possibly due to structural collapse at higher temperature. The mass loss temperature of CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP is lower than that of PS-DVB, confirming the presence of polymer layer in materials. The good thermal stability within 360°C was sufficient to ensure the widespread application of CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP in environment.

The adsorption kinetic process of CTFS by CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP were investigated within the range of 0–240 min ( Fig. 4A). The result showed that adsorption equilibrium of CPDs-NH2 @MIP for different concentration of CTFS (10, 20, and 50 mg L−1) can be achieved within 90 min, especially for the lower concentrations of CTFS (<20 mg L−1), which only takes 10 min to reach equilibrium. This is remarkably faster than the adsorption equilibrium time of those traditional adsorbents in literature ( Table 1)[56–59], which was usually ranged from 120–720 min. For CPDs-NH2@NIP, the adsorption curves exhibited similar trend to CPDs-NH2@MIP, while the adsorption equilibrium was reached approximately 100 min. Moreover, the adsorption capacity (Q
t ) of CPDs-NH2@NIP for different concentration of CTFS (1.56, 2.27 and 3.77 mg g−1) was obviously lower than that of CPDs-NH2@MIP (4.68, 6.94, and 12.34 mg g−1). This result indicated that there was a strong recognition driving force in CPDs-NH2@MIP, which was originated from the rich and precise binding sites formed by the involvement of spherical monomer CPDs-NH2. Therefore, the developed CPDs-NH2@MIP had faster mass transfer rate when adsorbing target, which was very beneficial for the adsorption and removal of low concentration pollutants.

The experimental data were further analyzed using the Pseudo-first-order, Pseudo-second-order kinetic, and Elovich model (see Supplementary Material), and the fitted kinetic parameters were listed in Table 2. From the result, it was found that the adsorption process of CTFS onto CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2 @NIP was better consistent with the Pseudo-second-order model compared to the other two models according to higher adjusted correlation coefficient (R
2
adj , 0.9579–0.9956) and lower standard deviation (SD, 0.0547–0.4598) [12,60], indicating that there were inhomogeneous binding sites. And the higher K
2 of CPDs-NH2@MIP illustrated that there existed a strong driving force, especially at lower concentrations (0.0150 g mg−1 min−1), which may come from the multiple precise imprinting sites and fewer non-imprinting regions brought by spherical monomers, effectively improving the adsorption process and enhancing the practicality of MIPs.

Adsorption isothermal fitting curves of imprinted polymers towards CTFS was presented in Fig. 4B at 25, 35 and 45°C, respectively. It was found that the adsorption capacity of CPDs-NH2@MIP for CTFS increased with the increase of initial solution concentration, and all Q values (28.23–25.18 mg g−1) were obviously higher than that of CPDs-NH2@NIP (8.46–5.87 mg g−1) within the experimental concentration range, which were 3.34, 3.40, and 4.29 times of those of CPDs-NH2@NIP at different temperature, respectively. The result indicated there was a strong driving force in CPDs-NH2@MIP for recognizing CTFS, which were from the imprinting effect. In contrast, the as-prepared CPDs-NH2@NIP using spherical functional monomer has a relatively dense structure, and almost all functional groups in CPDs-NH2 participated the polymerization process. And so there are no interacting binding sites in CPDs-NH2@NIP, and the lower adsorption capacity for CTFS only comes from the non-specific adsorption of itself.

Further, nonlinear fitting was employed to analyze the adsorption data using the Freundlich, Langmuir, and Liu adsorption isotherm models, respectively (see Supplementary Material), and Table 3 provided a summary of the results. It was found that the adsorption of CPDs-NH2@MIP for CTFS was better fitted by Liu isotherm model with the highest R
2
adj (0.9844–0.9972) and lower standard deviation (SD, 0.1271–0.4202). For CPDs-NH2@MIP, the fitted K
F values by the Freundlich model ranged from 1.3990 to 1.5044 were significantly higher than that of CPDs-NH2@NIP (0.1080–0.7482), illustrating that CPDs-NH2@MIP has a substantially higher adsorption capacity and a stronger affinity for template molecules compared to CPDs-NH2@NIP [61]. Moreover, the n
F in the Freundlich model represents the heterogeneity factor and the adsorption intensity, n
F > 1 represents a favorable adsorption condition, while the range of 1 to 10 signifies beneficial adsorption, showing suitable interaction between the adsorbate and the adsorbent. The n
F values were within 1.3273–2.1288 in this work, suggesting that there was a relatively strong adsorption intensity and favorable interaction between CPDs-NH2@MIP and CTFS [51,62]. The theoretical maximum adsorption capacity (Q
e ) of CTFS bound onto CPDs-NH2@MIP fitted by Liu isotherm model is 68.62 mg g−1. This adsorption capacity significantly surpassing the range of 7.10–78.70 mg g−1 for other traditional adsorbents in the literature (Table 1).

The adsorption nature of CTFS onto CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP was further discussed using the corresponding parameters, including ΔG
0 , ΔH
0 , and ΔS
0 (see Supplementary Material) at 25, 35, and 45°C, respectively, and presented in Table S4. All ΔG
0 values observed in this experiment were negative and exhibited a decreasing trend as the temperature rose. This suggests that the adsorption of CTFS onto CPDs-NH2@MIP is both spontaneous and achievable [63]. Moreover, the absolute value of ΔG
0 for CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP were separately in the range of 19.06–20.20 kJ mol−1 and 20.55–21.83 kJ mol−1. This finding implies that the process of CTFS adsorption onto these substances encompasses both physical and chemical adsorption mechanisms [64]. In addition, the negative ΔH
0 indicated that the binding is an exothermic process, and low temperature is beneficial for adsorption, which was more suitable for application in practical environmental systems. The positive ΔS
0 indicated CPDs-NH2@MIP displaced more small volume water molecules while adsorbing larger CTFS, leading to an increase in randomness in this system, and the adsorption process tends to proceed more flexible.

The single selectivity of CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP for CTFS and other substrate was investigated. As displayed in Fig. 4C, the CPDs-NH2@MIP has the strongest binding ability for CTFS, and the adsorption capacity (7.03 mg g−1) was highest among the six tested compounds, while the adsorption capacity for SMZ, SZ, OTC, TC, BPA, and AM was just 0.22–2.85 mg g−1. For CPDs-NH2@NIP, the adsorption capacity for all substrates was within 0.07–2.27 mg g−1, and the selective factor of αCTFS/SMZ, αCTFS/SZ, αCTFS/OTC, αCTFS/TC, αCTFS/BPA and αCTFS/AM were 2.42, 2.56, 3.18, 2.82, 4.75, and 33.27, respectively, indicating that CPDs-NH2@MIP has excellent adsorption for CTFS.

In order to value the selectivity of as-prepared imprinted material in complicated system, the binding of CPDs-NH2@MIP and CPDs-NH2@NIP towards CTFS in a mixing solution containing interference (CPL, CBZ, BPA, ATZ and BPB) was performed and the result was displayed in Fig. 4D. It can be seen that Q value of CPDs-NH2@MIP for CTFS was 6.68 mg g−1, which was more than 3.4 times that of other coexisting compounds (0.90–1.96 mg g−1). While the binding of CPDs-NH2@NIP for all the tested targets was very poor with just 0.09–1.19 mg g−1 with an imprinted factor of 5.61. Further, the selective parameters of K
d , K and K′ were calculated (see Supplementary Material). The result in Table S5 showed that K
d value (0.57 L g−1) of CPDs-NH2@MIP for CTFS was obviously higher than the other coexisting compounds (0.05–0.11), and the higher K values (>5.27) indicated that CPDs-NH2@MIP has good anti-interference against the coexisting substances in mixed system. Compared with CPDs-NH2@NIP, the selective performance K′ of CPDs-NH2@MIP for CTFS in a mixed system was higher than 7.05, indicating that the involvement of spherical monomers dramatically enhanced the selectivity of MIPs in complex system, which will greatly expand the application of MIPs in practical environmental samples.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of PS-DVB, CPDs-NH2@MIP, MAA@MIP, AA@MIP, and AMPS@MIP are displayed in Fig. 5A. Compared with the carrier PS-DVB, the four MIPs exhibited similar type IV isotherms, and there was hysteresis loop at the P/P0 ranged 0.4–0.9, indicating that there were mesoporous and microporous in polymers, which facilitated adsorption at a low relative pressure (P/P0) [65]. And the CPDs-NH2@MIP and MAA@MIP have similar adsorption volume, but the selective adsorption of CPDs-NH2@MIP for CTFS was significantly higher than the latter one, verifying that the imprinting site rather than pore volume affected the adsorption procedure. From Fig. 5B, it can be seen that the total pore volume of CPDs-NH2@NIP was lowest compared to the other three NIPs, illustrating that the involvement of spherical monomer made a denser structure of polymer, which can reduce the non-imprinted regions and non-specific adsorption.

The BET parameters summarized in Table S6 showed that the specific surface area of all imprinted materials decreased but the pore volume and mean pore diameter increased after the imprinted procedure on the surface of PS-DVB. Among them, the three parameters of CPDs-NH2@MIP were close to that of MAA@MIP, AA@MIP, and AMPS@MIP, suggesting that the remarkably enhanced adsorption of CPDs-NH2@MIP for CTFS was due to much more precisely matched imprinting sites. More importantly, the parameters of CPDs-NH2@NIP including specific surface area (195.1 m² g−1), pore volume (0.4818 cm³ g−1) and mean pore diameter (9.88 nm) were significantly lower than those of NIP prepared using traditional chain functional monomers, verified that the spherical monomer can fully interact with each other without template molecule, forming a denser structure and greatly reducing the non-imprinted regions. This result also indicated that the binding sites in CPDs-NH2@MIP are precise and ordered, which enhanced the adsorption selectivity. In contrast, the MIPs prepared using chain functional monomer cannot achieve this structure, and the inaccurate matching from steric hindrance led to self-aggregation of functional and the formation of ineffective non-imprinted cavities within the MIPs, resulting in poor selective recognition [66].

XPS spectra of CPDs-NH2@MIP showed that peaks with binding energies of 284.61 eV, 285.31 eV, 286.47 eV, and 287.84 eV were ascribed to CC, C–N, CN, and C–O, respectively (Fig. 5C-C1s) [67,68]. There are two prominent peaks in the O1s (Fig. 5C-O1s), centered at 531.86 eV and 533.08 eV, corresponding to the C-O and CO bonds, respectively [49,69], and the peaks at 399.92 eV, 400.52 eV and 402.35 eV in the N1s (Fig. 5C-N1s) was C−NH2, C−N−C and CN−C bonds, respectively [70]. After the adsorption of CTFS with abundant corresponding functional groups, the content of CN, CO, C−N − C and CN−C groups in CPDs-NH2@MIP separately increased from 7.23%, 49.74%, 51.05% and 5.26% to 12.92%, 63.58%, 53.17% and 7.45%, respectively. While the content of CN, CO, C−N−C and CN−C decreased to the original state after the elution of CTFS, respectively, indicating that there was weak interaction force between CPDs-NH2@MIP and imprinted molecule, which can achieve the completely adsorption and elution of target under mild conditions. In addition, after the adsorption of CTFS, the peaks of C–N (285.31 eV), CN (286.47 eV) and CO (533.08 eV) and CN−C (402.35 eV) of CPDs-NH2@MIP shifted to 285.44 eV, 286.65 eV, 532.81 eV and 400.76 eV, respectively, which verified the existing of hydrogen bonding interaction between the –NH2 group of CPDs-NH2 and the C–N, CN and CO groups in CTFS (Fig. S8) [10,71]. Besides, the FT-IR spectra of CTFS bound onto CPDs-NH2@MIP (Fig. S9) displayed that the groups of CO, –NH2, and –NH shifted from 1755 cm-1, 1530 cm-1, and 1351 cm-1, to 1758 cm-1, 1525 cm-1, and 1355 cm-1, respectively, verifying the hydrogen bonding interaction between CTFS and CPDs-NH2@MIP [72].

The effect of pH on the adsorption of imprinted material was investigated. As shown in Fig. S10, the binding of CPDs-NH2@MIP towards CTFS (Q=7.12–7.81 mg g−1, IF=3.82–5.75) was higher within the pH ranged from 4 to 10, and the adsorption performance was best at pH 6–7 (Q=8.62 mg g−1, IF=5.75), which was benefit for the adsorption of real samples. While the binding capacities were significantly decreased when the pH was higher than 10 and lower than 4. The possible reason was that there were −NH2 and −COOH groups in CTFS with two pK a values of 2.83 and 4.19, respectively. When the pH was beyond 4, CTFS exited in anionic form because of the deprotonation of carboxyl group in CTFS (Fig. S11) [73]. At this time, the zeta potential of CPDs-NH2@MIP was positive within pH 4 to 10 (Fig. S12), and so the electrostatic attraction occurred between CTFS and CPDs-NH2@MIP, which leads to an excellent adsorption. With the pH was beyond 10, the zeta potential of CPDs-NH2@MIP was negative, and the electrostatic repulsion between them caused the decrease of binding capacity. On the other hand, when pH< 4, the amine group in CTFS was partial protonated [74], and the electrostatic repulsion played a key role between CTFS with cationic states and CPDs-NH2@MIP with positive zeta potential, thereby bring a very poor binding.

All the above results demonstrated that more precise imprinted sites and fewer non-imprinted region generated from special spherical monomer CPDs-NH2, combined with hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions effectively enhanced the selectivity for target of CPDs-NH2@MIP in complex system.

The comparison of CPDs-NH2@MIP with commercial adsorbents including CNWBOND Si-SPE, CNWBOND LC-C18-SPE, CNW Poly-Sery-HLB-SPE, and Oasis Waters-SPE was performed. Towards this, a 10 mL mixture containing 10 mg L−1 of CTFS, CPL, CBZ, BPA, ATZ and BPB were passed through different SPE columns at a flow rate of 0.8 mL min−1, respectively, and the effluents were collected and determined by HPLC. The results in Fig. 6A showed that CPDs-NH2@MIP-SPE column can adsorb 100% of CTFS, while hardly adsorbing other coexisting substances, exhibiting excellent adsorption capacity and selectivity. However, all the commercial columns have no adsorption selectivity. For the Oasis Waters-SPE, it can adsorb almost all the tested compounds, and CNWBOND LC-C18-SPE, and CNW Poly-Sery-HLB-SPE can adsorb 21.28–98.23% of CTFS, CTFS, CPL, CBZ, BPA, ATZ and BPB. But only just 5.21–21.87% of all tested substrates can be adsorb by CNWBOND Si-SPE. This result indicated that these commercial adsorbents cannot selectively recognize and separate target from complex system.

Further, the other three SPE column packed with MIPs prepared with traditional chain functional monomers MAA (MAA@MIP), AA (AA@MIP) and AMPS (AMPS@MIP) were chosen to value the selectivity of CPDs-NH2@MIP. It can be seen (Fig. 6B) that MAA@MIP-SPE-column, AA@MIP-SPE-column, and AMPS@MIP-SPE-column not only adsorb template molecules well, but also almost completely adsorb tested substrates except for CPL, which illustrated that there is no significant difference in adsorption for substances with similar structures by the three traditional MIPs. In contrast, the CPDs-NH2@MIP-SPE column has different adsorption performance, and it can only adsorb more than 99% of CTFS, while hardly adsorb the other five coexisting substances (CPL, CBZ, BPA, ATZ and BPB), exhibiting superior selectivity. The precise binding sites generated by spherical functional monomer effectively enhanced the selectivity of CPDs-NH2@MIP in mixed solution, enabling it to exert better advantages in practical complex systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of real samples and reusability
ce:section.ce:para: Four water samples including river water, lake water, influent and effluent of sewage treatment plant and three food samples including milk, pork, and chicken were selected to investigate the application of CPDs-NH2@MIP in real samples. After loading 10 mL pretreated sample solution into the CPDs-NH2 @MIP-SPE column followed by elution with 1 mL acetonitrile, and each eluent was collected and analyzed by HPLC. The chromatograms in Fig. 6C, D, and E showed the CPDs-NH2@MIP-SPE column can effectively separate and enrich CTFS in real samples. The further recovery experiments ( Table 4) suggested that 81.88–122.15% of CTFS can be obtained from the spiked samples with RSD 1.28–5.12, proving the good practicality.

After CTFS adsorption onto the CPDs-NH2@MIP adsorbents, the adsorbents were regenerated with acetonitrile and subsequently with deionized water. In the subsequent cycle, the regenerated CPDs-NH2 @MIP adsorbents were reused for CTFS adsorption. As shown in Fig. S13, the adsorption performance did not significantly decline after 10 cycles, and the adsorption efficiency for CTFS remained higher than 98.2%, indicating that CPDs-NH2@MIP exhibited excellent reusability.

In this work, a unique nano-spherical amino-functionalized carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-NH2) was chosen as functional monomer to prepare a ceftiofur sodium (CTFS) imprinted material (CPDs-NH2 @MIP). The involvement of spherical monomer can achieve fully interaction with template molecule by overcoming the spatial hindrance, and both the affinity and selectivity of obtained CPDs-NH2@MIP was remarkably enhanced, which can selectively identify CTFS from a mixed system with a higher imprinting factor of 5.61 and reach adsorption equilibrium in just 10 min. The much more precise imprinting sites and fewer non-imprinted region enable CPDs-NH2@MIP to exhibit superior selectivity than commercial adsorbent and MIPs prepared with traditional chain functional monomer. Combined with the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions, the CPDs-NH2@MIP can highly selectively adsorb and remove 81.88–122.15% CTFS from real samples with at least 10 cycles. Hence, the usage of spherical functional monomer can overcome the limitation of traditional chain monomer and effectively improve the selectivity of MIP. This work will reveal the essence how the structure of functional monomers influence the selectivity of imprinted materials, and is expected to providing new ideas for the design of molecularly imprinted materials with highly selectivity in complex system.

Ceftiofur sodium (CTFS) antibiotic residues in the environment and food pose potential threats to ecosystems and human health; thus, developing highly selective and eco-friendly sample pretreatment technologies holds significant importance for the efficient recognition of CTFS. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) present a promising approach in the development of selective adsorbents for CTFS. However, conventional MIPs employing chain functional monomers demonstrate restricted imprinting factors and susceptibility to interference, thereby limiting their practical applicability. This work employs carbonized polymer dots-derived novel spherical functional monomers to fabricate MIPs with enhanced selectivity, which would inspire more ideas to implement in high-selectivity adsorbent construction.
